title: Drip vs Omnisend (2025): Which Is Better?
meta_description: Compare Drip and Omnisend email marketing platforms. See pricing, features, ratings, and which tool is best for your e-commerce business.
h1: Drip vs Omnisend: Detailed Comparison for E-commerce
intro: Both Drip and Omnisend are purpose-built email and SMS platforms for e-commerce businesses. This comparison helps you decide which tool offers the best fit for your store's marketing automation, budget, and growth stage.

comparison_table.tool_b: Free tier for up to 250 subscribers, paid plans from $16/month
Yes, free plan with limited features

comparison_table.tool_a: Visual automation builder, e-commerce tracking, revenue attribution
comparison_table.tool_b: Pre-built workflows, omnichannel automation, product picker

choose_a_if: You need deep e-commerce store integration with advanced revenue attribution tracking
You prefer a paid-only model without a free tier
Your primary focus is email and SMS without push notifications
choose_b_if: You want to start free and test before paying
You need omnichannel automation including push notifications
You prefer a lower entry price and higher customer satisfaction ratings
You want pre-built workflows to save setup time

faq.a: Neither is objectively better—it depends on your needs. Omnisend offers better value with a free tier, higher ratings, and more channels (including push notifications). Drip excels if you need advanced e-commerce tracking and revenue attribution. For most growing e-commerce stores, Omnisend provides more flexibility and features at a lower cost.

faq.q: Which is cheaper, Drip or Omnisend?
faq.a: Omnisend is significantly cheaper. It offers a free plan for up to 250 subscribers, while paid plans start at $16/month. Drip has no free tier and starts at $39/month. If you're budget-conscious or just starting out, Omnisend is the more affordable choice.

faq.q: Can I switch from Omnisend to Drip?
faq.a: Yes, both platforms support standard email export and data migration. Most e-commerce platforms like Shopify work with both tools, making migration straightforward. However, you may need to rebuild automation workflows from scratch, as the builders have different interfaces and features.
